_meta.description: Voice catalog for the Magalu PT-BR pilot (Fale com a Lu da Magalu). Three deliberately distinct premade voice profiles, chosen for distinct moments in the customer journey. In production, these must be re-evaluated against licensed Brazilian voice talent and a Professional Voice Clone (PVC) before any Lu-branded launch.
_meta.notes: Cleaned for reviewer consistency: Luiza uses the Rachel premade voice as the Phase 1 bridge voice; production Lu requires licensed talent and PVC. Marina uses Matilda; Rafael uses Adam.

voices.name: warm_female_br
voices.voice_id_source_voice: Rachel (ElevenLabs premade, current library 2026-05). Selected after auditioning alternatives because Rachel gives the Luiza bridge voice a warmer, slightly more playful PT-BR register for support and notification flows.
voices.description: Warm, conversational female voice. Use for greetings, balance confirmations, anything where the customer needs to feel met by a person, not processed by a system.
voices.rationale: Brazilian retail customer service has a strong cultural expectation of warmth and tratamento humanizado. Rachel on eleven_multilingual_v2 gives the Phase 1 prototype a soft, conversational bridge voice that can test WhatsApp voice-notification behavior without implying that a premade voice is the production Lu. Luiza is a prototype voice; the production Lu voice should be a Phase 2 Professional Voice Clone (PVC) of approved Brazilian voice talent, subject to licensing and brand approval.
voices.tradeoffs: Not ideal for high-friction moments such as denials, fraud holds, or delivery exceptions where a calmer escalation pattern may work better. Also slightly slower than a power user wants for repeat status checks; keep utterances short and let users interrupt.

voices.name: neutral_male_br
voices.description: Neutral, efficient male voice. Use for transactional confirmations, high-stakes delivery exceptions, return-process readbacks, refund-denial communication: moments where clarity beats warmth.
voices.rationale: When a Magalu customer is anxious (order issue, delivery exception, return processing under CDC scrutiny), they want competence signaled fast. A grounded mid-register male voice reads as institutional credibility in BR retail, the audio equivalent of the store manager who looks you in the eye. Adam's stability score in eleven_multilingual_v2 is high, which matters for read-back of order numbers, prices, and tracking codes where TTS artifacts erode trust. Used surgically in the demo surface to illustrate persona-switching for high-stakes retail moments; never the production Lu voice (using Rafael as Lu's primary would break the brand entirely). Note: Rafael is NOT used in the Magalu Phase 1 deployment, which is Luiza-only for outbound notifications. Reserved as a cross-vertical reference and for the optional comparison surface only.
voices.tradeoffs: Reads as cold for soft moments (welcome, thanks, closing). Should never be used as the agent's default voice: it would make Magalu feel like a 1990s call center. Use surgically.

voices.name: energetic_female_br
voices.description: Bright, energetic female voice. Use for proactive notifications, marketing-adjacent confirmations (cashback earned, goal reached), onboarding moments.
voices.rationale: Brazilian retail loves positive emotional reinforcement: "seu pedido foi enviado," "você ganhou um cupom," "sua entrega chegou." Matilda's warm upper-mid register with a naturally upbeat intonation in eleven_multilingual_v2 reads as celebratory without sounding like a stadium announcer. She replaces Bella (deprecated from the ElevenLabs premade library, 2026-05); Lily was too calm and Alice too newsreader-formal for the celebratory beats this voice owns. Reserved for positive-affect moments outside the support flow (order shipped, Magalu Ouro reward earned, Magalu Pay savings milestone). Pre-selected as the celebration-cell voice in Phase 2 A/B testing; out of scope for Phase 1.
voices.tradeoffs: Inappropriate for service recovery, fraud, or any moment where the customer is stressed : the energy will feel tone-deaf. Also can sound performative on long passages; keep utterances under ~15 seconds when using this voice. Not suitable as the agent's escalation voice. Because Matilda is an American-English premade, on PT-BR the multilingual model occasionally leaks a faint English vowel color on stressed syllables : re-roll if it becomes audible on a given take.
